label: Early American Republic
pitch:
The gunsmoke of Yorktown has barely cleared when a new crisis begins: how do you actually run a country built on the idea that nobody should have too much power? The answer nearly tears the republic apart before it's a decade old.

From 1783 to 1815, a handful of brilliant, flawed, feuding men invent American democracy in real time. Washington sets precedents by instinct. Hamilton builds a financial system so audacious it enrages half the country. Jefferson preaches small government — then doubles the nation's size with a handshake and a check. Adams signs laws that criminalize criticizing the president. And the whole experiment nearly ends when Britain burns the White House to the ground.

🔑 Key Facts:
• The Articles of Confederation were so weak that Congress couldn't even collect taxes — Shays' Rebellion proved the government couldn't keep order in its own states
• Hamilton's financial plan assumed $75 million in state debts, created a national bank, and restored American credit — in exchange for moving the capital to a mosquito swamp on the Potomac
• The Louisiana Purchase doubled the nation's size for about three cents an acre, but Jefferson had to abandon his own strict-constructionist principles to do it
• The War of 1812 ended in a draw, but Andrew Jackson's victory at New Orleans — fought two weeks after the peace treaty was signed — made Americans feel they'd won a second independence

🤔 Why It Matters: Every institution Americans take for granted — the presidency, the Supreme Court, the two-party system, judicial review, the peaceful transfer of power — was improvised during these 32 years. The founders didn't follow a blueprint; they argued, compromised, and sometimes betrayed their own principles. The decisions made in Philadelphia parlors and Virginia dining rooms still shape American life today.

🎲 Wild Card: When the British burned Washington in 1814, First Lady Dolley Madison refused to flee until she'd cut Gilbert Stuart's full-length portrait of George Washington from its frame and smuggled it out. The painting hangs in the White House today — the only object that has been there since 1800.

details.label: Background
details.text: After independence, the thirteen states were bound only by the Articles of Confederation — a deliberately weak compact that gave Congress no power to tax, no executive to enforce laws, and no courts to resolve disputes between states.

details.label: What Happened
details.text: Shays' Rebellion in 1786 — farmers storming Massachusetts courthouses over debt — shocked the nation's leaders into action. Delegates convened in Philadelphia in 1787 and, behind closed doors, scrapped the Articles entirely and drafted the Constitution.

details.label: The Bigger Picture
details.text: The Confederation Era proved that winning independence was easier than building a nation. The failure of the Articles forced a fundamental rethinking of how sovereignty, liberty, and union could coexist — a debate that still echoes in American politics.

details.label: Background
details.text: The Constitution created a framework, but the new federal government had no precedents, no bureaucracy, and no money. Everything Washington and Congress did in 1789 set a template — from cabinet departments to the two-term tradition.

details.label: What Happened
details.text: Hamilton built a financial system from scratch: national bank, federal assumption of state debts, and a tariff structure. Jefferson and Madison opposed him at every step, and by the mid-1790s two rival parties — Federalists and Democratic-Republicans — had crystallized around the dispute.

details.label: The Bigger Picture
details.text: The Federalist Era answered whether a republic could govern itself in peacetime. It also created America's first ideological fault line: strong central government versus states' rights, a tension that would define every political era to follow.

details.label: Background
details.text: Jefferson's election in 1800 was the first peaceful transfer of power between rival parties in modern history — a milestone that was far from guaranteed. Federalists feared the Democratic-Republicans would dismantle the federal institutions Hamilton had built.

details.label: What Happened
details.text: Jefferson doubled the nation's size with the Louisiana Purchase in 1803, then sent Lewis and Clark to map it. The War of 1812 — sparked by British impressment of American sailors — nearly ended in disaster before Andrew Jackson's victory at New Orleans turned it into a rallying triumph.

details.label: The Bigger Picture
details.text: The Jeffersonian Era showed that the republic could survive a change in governing philosophy and a second war with Britain. It also planted the seeds of American expansionism and the westward drive that would define the next half-century.

events.label: Shays' Rebellion
events.description: Debt-ridden Massachusetts farmers led by Daniel Shays took up arms against state courts that were seizing their property. The federal government under the Articles of Confederation was powerless to respond, proving the need for a stronger central government.

events.label: Constitutional Convention
events.description: Fifty-five delegates met in Philadelphia, ostensibly to fix the Articles of Confederation. Behind closed doors, with windows nailed shut in the summer heat, they wrote an entirely new constitution with a stronger federal government, separation of powers, and a system of checks and balances.

events.label: U.S. Constitution ratified
events.description: The Constitution established a federal government with separation of powers and checks and balances. It remains the oldest written national constitution still in use.

events.label: Federalist Papers published
events.description: Hamilton, Madison, and Jay published 85 essays arguing for ratification of the Constitution. The Federalist Papers remain the most authoritative commentary on the American constitutional system.

events.label: Washington inaugurated as President
events.description: George Washington was unanimously elected the first President, setting precedents for the office including a peaceful transfer of power. His voluntary retirement after two terms became a lasting tradition.

events.label: Hamilton's financial system
events.description: Alexander Hamilton established a national bank, federal assumption of state debts, and an excise tax. His financial system gave the young republic economic credibility and a functioning government.

events.label: Bill of Rights ratified
events.description: The first ten amendments guaranteed fundamental freedoms including speech, religion, press, and the right to bear arms. They addressed Anti-Federalist concerns about federal overreach.

events.label: Haitian Revolution
events.description: The only successful large-scale slave revolt in history established Haiti as the first free Black republic. It terrified Southern slaveholders and directly influenced Napoleon's decision to sell Louisiana — his Caribbean empire had collapsed.

events.label: Eli Whitney's cotton gin
events.description: Whitney's machine could clean 50 pounds of cotton per day, making short-staple cotton hugely profitable. The invention entrenched slavery in the Southern economy — cotton production grew from 3,000 bales in 1790 to 4.5 million by 1860.

events.label: Whiskey Rebellion
events.description: Western Pennsylvania farmers revolted against Hamilton's excise tax on whiskey. Washington personally led 13,000 militia to suppress the uprising — the first test of federal authority under the Constitution.

events.label: John Adams presidency
events.description: Adams inherited a quasi-war with France and deepening partisan divisions. He avoided full-scale war through diplomacy but signed the controversial Alien and Sedition Acts. His peaceful transfer of power to Jefferson in 1801 proved the republic could survive a change of ruling party.

events.label: Alien and Sedition Acts
events.description: President Adams signed four laws that criminalized 'false, scandalous, and malicious' criticism of the government and gave the president power to deport non-citizens. The acts provoked a constitutional crisis and helped doom Adams's reelection.

events.label: White House completed
events.description: John Adams became the first president to occupy the Executive Mansion in the new capital of Washington, D.C. The building — designed by Irish-born architect James Hoban — was largely built by enslaved laborers.

events.label: Jefferson elected President
events.description: Thomas Jefferson's election marked the first peaceful transfer of power between opposing parties. His presidency championed agrarian democracy and limited federal government.

events.label: Whitney's interchangeable parts
events.description: Eli Whitney demonstrated muskets assembled from interchangeable parts to President Adams and Congress. Though his initial demonstration may have been staged, the concept revolutionized manufacturing and laid the groundwork for the American system of mass production.

events.label: First Barbary War
events.description: The United States fought the Barbary States of North Africa to end tribute payments and protect American merchant ships. The war gave the young republic its first overseas military victory and the Marine Corps hymn its famous line 'to the shores of Tripoli.'

events.label: Cane Ridge Revival
events.description: An estimated 20,000 people gathered in rural Kentucky for a week-long camp meeting that ignited the Second Great Awakening. The revival democratized American religion, spawning new denominations and fueling reform movements from temperance to abolition.

events.label: Marbury v. Madison
events.description: Chief Justice John Marshall established the principle of judicial review — the Supreme Court's power to strike down laws that violate the Constitution. This case made the judiciary a co-equal branch of government and remains the foundation of American constitutional law.

events.label: Louisiana Purchase
events.description: Jefferson purchased 828,000 square miles from France for $15 million, doubling the size of the United States. It was the largest land deal in history and opened the West to expansion.

events.label: Lewis and Clark Expedition
events.description: Meriwether Lewis and William Clark explored the newly acquired western territory to the Pacific Ocean. Their expedition mapped the route and established American claims to the Oregon Country.

events.label: Fulton's steamboat Clermont
events.description: Robert Fulton's Clermont made the first commercially successful steamboat voyage, traveling 150 miles up the Hudson River in 32 hours. Steamboats transformed river transportation and opened the interior to rapid commerce.

events.label: War of 1812
events.description: The United States fought Britain over trade restrictions and impressment of American sailors. The war ended in a draw but cemented American sovereignty and national identity.

events.label: Treaty of Ghent
events.description: American and British diplomats signed a peace treaty in Belgium that ended the War of 1812 with no territorial changes. News traveled so slowly that Andrew Jackson's famous victory at New Orleans was fought two weeks after peace had already been agreed.

events.label: Stamp Act
events.description: Parliament taxed printed paper in the colonies, from newspapers to legal documents to playing cards. The objection was not the cost but the principle: a free people could only be taxed by a legislature in which they had a voice, compressed into the slogan 'no taxation without representation.'

events.label: Boston Massacre
events.description: British soldiers fired into a jeering, snowball-throwing Boston crowd and killed five men. The colonists turned the street confrontation into propaganda against imperial rule.

events.label: Boston Tea Party
events.description: Members of the Sons of Liberty, some disguised as Native men, boarded three ships in Boston Harbor and threw 342 chests of British tea into the water rather than let the taxed cargo be landed. Britain's furious response, the Intolerable Acts, closed the port and rewrote the Massachusetts charter.

events.label: Intolerable Acts
events.description: Parliament's punitive laws closed the port of Boston until the tea was paid for and transferred decisions from Massachusetts's elected bodies to Crown-controlled officials. The acts gave the quarreling colonies a common enemy and produced the First Continental Congress.

events.label: First Continental Congress
events.description: An improvised, self-appointed assembly with no legal authority, thirteen delegations met in Philadelphia, agreed to act together, and enforced a shared trade boycott against Britain.

events.label: Lexington and Concord
events.description: British troops and Massachusetts militia exchanged fire, turning a constitutional argument into a war. For more than a year the colonies fought while still formally claiming to be loyal subjects.

events.label: Paine's Common Sense
events.description: Thomas Paine's ferociously plain-spoken pamphlet argued that monarchy was absurd and the colonies should declare independence. It sold over 100,000 copies in a population of 2.5 million and moved public opinion from reconciliation toward independence.

events.label: Declaration of Independence
events.description: Drafted mainly by Thomas Jefferson and adopted by the Continental Congress, the Declaration asserted that 'all men are created equal' and that government rests on the consent of the governed. The sentence the man who wrote it contradicted by owning more than one hundred enslaved people is the spine of the early republic's story.

events.label: Thomas Paine arrives in America
events.description: An unsuccessful English corset-maker and customs officer, Paine arrived nearly penniless with a letter from Benjamin Franklin and a gift for plain, furious prose that would transform the independence debate.

events.label: Thomas Jefferson drafts the Declaration
events.description: A thirty-three-year-old Virginia planter and lawyer already famous in Virginia as a political pamphleteer, Jefferson was handed the pen because his colleagues considered him the best prose stylist in the room.

events.label: Battle of Saratoga
events.description: An entire British army of around 6,000 men under General John Burgoyne was surrounded and surrendered in upstate New York. Saratoga was the proof France wanted that the Americans could actually win.

events.label: Franco-American Alliance
events.description: France signed a formal alliance with the United States, committing the two countries to fight Britain together, neither making a separate peace. It converted a colonial rebellion into a global war and was the single most decisive event of the Revolution.

events.label: Lafayette joins the Continental Army
events.description: A French aristocrat barely twenty years old and immensely rich, the Marquis de Lafayette paid his own way to America, served without pay, and became so close to the childless Washington that the two treated each other as father and son.

events.label: Valley Forge winter encampment
events.description: The Continental Army's brutal winter camp where Baron von Steuben drilled a brave but untrained mass into a force that could march in step, form a battle line, and maneuver as a unit instead of an armed crowd.

events.label: Von Steuben's drill manual
events.description: A former officer in the Prussian army, Baron von Steuben drilled the Continental Army in person and through a manual he wrote, transforming its battlefield discipline.

events.label: British Southern Campaign
events.description: Britain shifted its main effort to the southern colonies on the theory that Loyalists were more numerous there. It became a brutal internal war of neighbor against neighbor, and it turned partly on the tens of thousands of enslaved people who fled to British lines on the promise of freedom.

events.label: Siege of Yorktown
events.description: A French fleet shut the sea door at Chesapeake Bay while Washington's and Rochambeau's armies shut the land door, trapping General Cornwallis. His surrender of roughly eight thousand men broke Britain's political will to continue the war.

events.label: Treaty of Paris
events.description: Franklin, Adams, and Jay quietly disobeyed their own government's instructions and negotiated separately with Britain. The treaty recognized full American independence and set the western boundary at the Mississippi River, roughly doubling the territory.

events.label: Loyalist exodus
events.description: Somewhere on the order of sixty thousand to a hundred thousand colonists who had stayed loyal to the Crown left the United States permanently for Canada, Britain, or the Caribbean, one of the largest forced political exoduses of the eighteenth century.

events.label: Pennsylvania gradual abolition law
events.description: The first gradual abolition law freed no one already enslaved; it declared children born to enslaved mothers technically free but bound to serve the mother's owner until age twenty-eight. It set the slow Northern pattern that ended slavery over decades.

events.label: Sullivan Campaign
events.description: Washington ordered General John Sullivan to destroy the Iroquois ability to make war: the campaign systematically burned roughly forty Iroquois towns, orchards, and winter food stores, driving thousands of refugees into a deadly winter. The Iroquois named Washington 'Town Destroyer.'

events.label: Newburgh Conspiracy
events.description: Anonymous papers circulated among unpaid Continental officers hinting that the army should refuse to disband or even march on Congress. Washington walked into the meeting unannounced and, fumbling for his spectacles, talked the mutiny down without a shot.

events.label: Washington resigns his commission
events.description: With the war won and his prestige absolute, Washington went to Congress, formally surrendered his commission as commander-in-chief, and went home to his farm. He gave up power voluntarily when he could almost certainly have kept it, establishing that in this republic the soldier serves the state and steps aside.

events.label: Articles of Confederation ratified
events.description: The first written framework of government for the United States was a treaty of friendship among thirteen small countries: no president, no national courts, one vote per state, and amendment requiring unanimous consent. The weakness was deliberate, designed by men terrified of recreating a distant, coercive central power.

events.label: Land Ordinance of 1785
events.description: It set up the system for surveying and selling western territory: a grid of square six-mile townships subdivided into thirty-six one-mile sections, with one section per township reserved to fund public schools. The Confederation's surveyors drew the country's literal shape.

events.label: Northwest Ordinance
events.description: It created a template by which a western territory would pass through stages and be admitted to the Union as a fully equal state, not a permanent colony, and it banned slavery in the entire Northwest Territory, drawing the first federal line between where slavery could and could not go.

events.label: Barbary states seize American sailors
events.description: With no navy and no money to pay tribute, the independent United States could not protect its merchant sailors from the North African Barbary states, who seized ships and held crews for ransom or enslaved them.

events.label: Philadelphia Convention
events.description: Fifty-five men met in the building where independence had been declared, closed the windows in the heat, swore themselves to secrecy, and over four months wrote an entirely new framework of government with no legal authority to do so.

events.label: Madison's Virginia Plan
events.description: A small, frail, brilliant Virginian who had read deeply in the history of failed confederations, James Madison arrived best prepared with a worked-out plan for a strong national government of three checking branches and a theory that a large republic is safer because so many factions check each other.

events.label: The Great Compromise
events.description: Brokered by Roger Sherman of Connecticut, it gave Congress two houses: a House of Representatives apportioned by population and a Senate with two seats per state. It used bicameralism to balance states against each other and saved the deadlocked Convention.

events.label: Three-Fifths Clause
events.description: Each enslaved person was counted as three-fifths of a person for apportioning representation and direct taxes. It did not make enslaved people partly free; it gave slaveholding states extra seats in Congress and extra electoral votes in proportion to the human beings they held in bondage.

events.label: Slave-trade clause
events.description: A constitutional clause forbade Congress from banning the Atlantic slave trade for twenty years, until 1808, the price of South Carolina and Georgia joining at all.

events.label: The Electoral College
events.description: A deliberately indirect, anti-majoritarian device: each state gets electors equal to its total seats in Congress and decides for itself how to pick them, and the electors, not voters directly, cast the votes for president.

events.label: Invention of the Presidency
events.description: The Convention built, almost from nothing, a single elected chief executive serving a fixed four-year term, commander of the military but unable to declare war, able to veto laws subject to override, and removable by impeachment. Every power was fenced by a counter-power held elsewhere.

events.label: The Federalist published
events.description: Under the shared pen name 'Publius,' Hamilton, Madison, and Jay wrote eighty-five newspaper essays for popular persuasion. They were get-out-the-vote campaign documents and remain the deepest explanation of how the constitutional machine was meant to work.

events.label: Anti-Federalist opposition
events.description: Opponents of the Constitution argued a large republic with a strong central government would slide toward tyranny and demanded a written list of protected liberties. They lost the war but extracted the Bill of Rights as the price.

events.label: Virginia ratifies the Constitution
events.description: The largest state ratified narrowly, 89 to 79, after a ferocious debate between Madison for the Constitution and the Revolutionary orator Patrick Henry against it.

events.label: Patrick Henry opposes ratification
events.description: Virginia's most famous Revolutionary orator, whose 'give me liberty or give me death' speech had helped push the colony toward war in 1775, turned the same thunder against a national government he feared would swallow the states.

events.label: Federalist No. 10
events.description: Madison's argument that a large republic controls faction better than a small one, because over a huge territory so many competing interests arise that no single faction can easily seize a permanent majority. It is the intellectual core of the American experiment.

events.label: Federalist No. 51
events.description: Madison's answer to the objection that the people running government may not be good men: arrange the offices so each official's ambition checks every other's. 'Ambition must be made to counteract ambition,' because 'if men were angels, no government would be necessary.'

events.label: Washington's first inauguration
events.description: On April 30, 1789, Washington took the oath on a balcony in New York City for a job no human being had ever held. Chosen unanimously by the Electoral College, he knew every single thing he did would set a precedent.

events.label: Washington's first Cabinet
events.description: The Constitution did not mention a cabinet; Washington created one by appointing and regularly consulting Jefferson at State, Hamilton at Treasury, Knox at War, and Randolph as Attorney General. Governing through a council of chosen department heads is a Washington invention, not a constitutional command.

events.label: The presidential title fight
events.description: Some senators, led by Vice President John Adams, wanted a grand title like 'His Highness the President.' Washington let the plain 'Mr. President' win, because a republican chief magistrate addressed like a monarch starts becoming one.

events.label: Judiciary Act of 1789
events.description: It built a whole branch of government the Constitution had barely sketched: it set the Supreme Court's size, created lower federal courts, and defined the cases the Court could hear. One provision would later be struck down in the case that invented judicial review.

events.label: First Federal Congress
events.description: Doing work so foundational historians call it a second constitutional convention, it created the executive departments, passed revenue laws giving the government an actual income, sent out the Bill of Rights, and passed the Judiciary Act.

events.label: Report on Public Credit
events.description: Hamilton's audacious plan to fund the debt at par, paying old IOUs at full face value, on the reasoning that a government that always pays in full can always borrow cheaply in the next emergency.

events.label: Federal assumption of state debts
events.description: Hamilton proposed the federal government take over and pay the states' war debts as well as its own, deliberately attaching the financial self-interest of a nation-spanning creditor class to the survival of the national government.

events.label: The Compromise of 1790
events.description: At a single private dinner hosted by Jefferson, Madison agreed to let assumption pass and Hamilton agreed to put the permanent capital on the Potomac. The financial system and the capital's location were traded for each other over a meal.

events.label: First Bank of the United States
events.description: A national bank, partly government-owned, to hold federal money, issue a stable currency, and lend to government and business. It provoked the first great constitutional fight over the limits of federal power, strict versus loose construction.

events.label: Report on Manufactures
events.description: Hamilton's most radical state paper argued the federal government should deliberately build American industry from scratch with protective tariffs and subsidies. Congress declined most of it, but it became the blueprint for Clay's later American System.

events.label: Birth of the first party system
events.description: By the early 1790s the founders had sorted into Federalists around Hamilton, favoring strong central government and commerce, and Democratic-Republicans around Jefferson and Madison, favoring states' rights and agriculture. The framers had not wanted parties; they formed anyway.

events.label: The French Revolution splits America
events.description: The French Revolution turned foreign policy into partisan passion: Democratic-Republicans saw France as the embattled sister republic, Federalists saw bloody anarchy and championed orderly Britain. Each side believed the other was selling the republic to a foreign tyranny.

events.label: Citizen Genêt affair
events.description: The French Republic's minister Edmond-Charles Genêt began recruiting Americans into privateering ventures on American soil, appealing directly to the public over the government's head, an open challenge to whether the United States controlled its own foreign policy.

events.label: Proclamation of Neutrality
events.description: Washington declared the United States would take neither side in the European war and forbade citizens from participating. The precedent that the president leads in foreign affairs largely dates from this unilateral act.

events.label: Jay's Treaty
events.description: John Jay's treaty with Britain got the British forts on American soil evacuated and avoided a war the country could not afford, but conceded almost everything else and won nothing on impressment. It triggered the most violent partisan eruption of the era and hardened the two parties into permanent machines.

events.label: Pinckney's Treaty
events.description: The American envoy to a distracted, declining Spain came away with almost everything: it fixed the southwestern boundary and guaranteed American farmers the right to ship crops down the Mississippi through Spanish-held New Orleans, the lifeline of the western economy.

events.label: Washington's Farewell Address
events.description: Released to a newspaper rather than delivered aloud, Washington's open letter warned against the 'spirit of party' and 'permanent alliances' with Europe. It shaped American foreign policy for over a century, written by a man watching the partisan split he feared.

events.label: Democratic-Republican Party forms
events.description: Built around Jefferson and Madison, favoring states' rights, agriculture, France, and a narrow reading of federal power, the party was the ancestor of today's Democratic Party (not today's Republicans). It proved the machinery could absorb deep organized disagreement without breaking.

events.label: Quasi-War with France
events.description: An undeclared naval conflict, never formally a war: France, furious over Jay's Treaty, seized American merchant ships in large numbers, and the small U.S. Navy fought back at sea.

events.label: XYZ Affair
events.description: French intermediaries, named in the dispatches only X, Y, and Z, demanded a bribe and a loan before talks could begin. When Adams released the dispatches, 'Millions for defense, but not one cent for tribute' swept the country and the Federalists won big.

events.label: Naturalization Act of 1798
events.description: It raised the residency required to become a citizen from five to fourteen years, aimed squarely at immigrants, who tended to vote Republican.

events.label: The Sedition Act
events.description: It made it a federal crime to write or speak 'false, scandalous, and malicious' statements against the government. In practice it was used to jail Republican newspaper editors and a Republican congressman, eight years after the First Amendment was ratified.

events.label: Kentucky and Virginia Resolutions
events.description: Secretly written by Jefferson and Madison and passed by friendly state legislatures, they argued a state may declare a federal law void within its borders. This is the doctrine of nullification, drawn first in the cause of free speech and later, fatefully, in the cause of slavery.

events.label: Adams sends a peace commission to France
events.description: Over the open opposition of his own party and at fatal political cost, Adams sent a new peace commission to Paris, ended the Quasi-War by negotiation, and prevented a war that would have been wildly popular but might have ruined the republic.

events.label: Election of 1800
events.description: A campaign of genuine viciousness ended with the party controlling the presidency, Congress, and the courts losing to a party it had called traitors and atheists, and then handing over power. Measured against all of recorded history, the peaceful transfer was one of the rarest things that had ever happened.

events.label: The Jefferson–Burr tie
events.description: A constitutional flaw let Jefferson and his running mate Aaron Burr tie with identical electoral votes, throwing the election into a House still controlled by defeated Federalists. The deadlock broke after thirty-six ballots, largely because Hamilton waged a private campaign against Burr.

events.label: Twelfth Amendment
events.description: It required electors to cast separate, labeled votes for president and vice president, patching the structural bug that nearly crashed the machine in 1801.

events.label: Jefferson's first inaugural address
events.description: Into the uncertain quiet, Jefferson said 'We are all republicans, we are all federalists,' asserting both parties operated within one constitutional consensus and the loser need not fear the winner. It is the founding statement of the idea of a loyal opposition.

events.label: Aaron Burr
events.description: A brilliant, charming, and slippery New York politician put on the Republican ticket to help carry that crucial state, Burr nearly stole the presidency in the 1801 deadlock and four years later killed Hamilton in a duel.

events.label: John Marshall becomes Chief Justice
events.description: A Virginia Federalist and Revolutionary War veteran named Chief Justice by Adams in his last weeks, Marshall would plant a strong Federalist mind in the one branch elections could not sweep out and invent judicial review.

events.label: The midnight judges
events.description: The lame-duck Federalist Congress created a batch of new judgeships and Adams filled them in his last hours to entrench Federalist control of the judiciary. One undelivered commission produced Marbury v. Madison.

events.label: Jeffersonian retrenchment
events.description: Jefferson walked to his own inauguration, slashed the federal budget, shrank the army and navy, let the Sedition Act expire, pardoned its prisoners, and repealed the internal taxes including the whiskey excise, changing the register of government to deliberate, frugal smallness.

events.label: Spain suspends the New Orleans deposit
events.description: When Spain suspended the Americans' right to deposit goods at New Orleans, the West was enraged and war talk spread, because every western farmer floated his crops down the Mississippi to be loaded at that port.

events.label: Jefferson abandons strict construction
events.description: By his own strict-constructionist principles, Jefferson believed the Purchase required a constitutional amendment, but he swallowed his doctrine and justified it under the treaty power. The strict-constructionist president performed the largest loose-construction act in the country's history.

events.label: Sacagawea guides Lewis and Clark
events.description: A teenaged Shoshone woman captured as a child and sold, traveling with a French trader and her own infant, Sacagawea's interpreting and diplomacy with mountain nations repeatedly kept the expedition alive.

events.label: Monticello
events.description: Jefferson's famous house, built and run on the labor of the more than six hundred people he enslaved over his life, and the setting of the contested question of his relationship with Sally Hemings.

events.label: The Sally Hemings question
events.description: Published in Jefferson's lifetime and used against him politically, the claim that he fathered children with the enslaved Sally Hemings was denied for two centuries as slander. A 1998 DNA study plus the documentary record led the institution that runs Monticello and most historians to conclude Jefferson was the father.

events.label: The Second Slavery
events.description: Not the colonial institution slowly fading but a new, modern, expanding, brutally profitable system integrated into the global industrial economy, that the cotton gin made possible exactly when slavery had looked vulnerable.

events.label: Eli Whitney
events.description: A Connecticut-born tinkerer visiting a Georgia plantation, Whitney built the cotton gin and later championed interchangeable parts, two inventions that reorganized the American economy in opposite moral directions.

events.label: The domestic slave trade
events.description: On the order of a million enslaved people were forcibly moved from the Upper South to the cotton frontier of the Deep South, marched in coffles chained at the neck, sold as ordinary commerce with families broken up by sale. 'Sold down the river' comes from exactly this.

events.label: Atlantic slave trade banned
events.description: Congress banned the importation of enslaved people from Africa effective January 1, 1808, the earliest constitutionally possible date. It ended American participation in the Atlantic trade but, because the enslaved population grew by natural increase, functioned in the economy like a protective tariff on American-born enslaved people.

events.label: The Cotton Kingdom
events.description: By 1820 the single most dynamic sector of the American economy was slave-grown cotton for the world's factories, the cotton frontier moving relentlessly southwest into Alabama, Mississippi, Louisiana, and eventually Texas.

events.label: Second Great Awakening
events.description: A wave of intense religious revival shifted American Protestantism away from Calvinist predestination toward the idea that any individual could choose salvation. Its social fuse was reform: temperance, public education, prison reform, and, most explosively, abolitionism.

events.label: African Methodist Episcopal Church founded
events.description: Richard Allen, a formerly enslaved Methodist preacher pulled from his knees and ordered to a segregated gallery while praying, walked out and founded the first fully independent Black denomination in the United States.

events.label: Disestablishment of state churches
events.description: Over these decades the states, one by one, ended tax support and legal privilege for any denomination (Massachusetts last, in 1833), producing a society in which religion was entirely voluntary, an institutional invention running opposite to the European norm.

events.label: Slater's Mill
events.description: Samuel Slater, a British mechanic, slipped out of England disguised as a farm laborer with the spinning machinery memorized in his head and rebuilt it in Rhode Island. American industrialization began as industrial espionage.

events.label: Erie Canal begun
events.description: New York began digging a man-made waterway across the state linking the Hudson and the Atlantic to the Great Lakes. When it opened it cut shipping costs to the interior by roughly ninety percent and pulled the West economically toward the Northern seaboard.

events.label: Lancaster Turnpike
events.description: A privately built toll road running about sixty miles from Philadelphia to Lancaster proved people would pay to use a good hard road and touched off a wave of turnpike building.

events.label: The Napoleonic Wars squeeze American shipping
events.description: The continent-spanning death struggle between Napoleon's France, dominating the land, and Britain, dominating the sea, crushed neutral American commerce as each power tried to strangle the other's trade.

events.label: British impressment of American sailors
events.description: The British Navy stopped American ships at sea and seized sailors it claimed were British subjects, forcing them into its service. Thousands of genuine American citizens were taken; to Americans it was a direct assault on national sovereignty.

events.label: Chesapeake–Leopard affair
events.description: The British warship Leopard demanded to search the U.S. Navy frigate Chesapeake for deserters, opened fire when refused, killed and wounded American sailors, and removed four men, an attack on a naval vessel in peacetime.

events.label: Embargo of 1807
events.description: Rather than fight, Jefferson banned virtually all American ships from sailing to foreign ports, betting economic pain would force respect for American rights. It devastated Americans far more than Britain, left grass growing on idle wharves, and was repealed as a near-total failure.

events.label: The War Hawks
events.description: A new generation of young, aggressive, mostly Southern and Western congressmen led by Henry Clay argued submission was destroying national honor and that war with Britain might also let the United States seize Canada and end British support for Native resistance.

events.label: Henry Clay enters Congress
events.description: A charismatic, hard-drinking frontier lawyer and planter from Kentucky in his early thirties, already Speaker of the House and the most magnetic orator of his generation, Clay dominates the last third of this book and the next.

events.label: Tecumseh's confederacy
events.description: A Shawnee war leader, Tecumseh built a pan-Indian movement on a radical argument: no single chief could sell land because it belonged to all Native peoples in common, and the only defense was for the nations to unite and stop selling.

events.label: Tenskwatawa, the Prophet
events.description: Tecumseh's brother, called the Prophet after a dramatic religious vision, preached a spiritual revival, a return to Native ways and rejection of American goods and whiskey, from a base at Prophetstown on the Tippecanoe River.

events.label: Battle of Tippecanoe
events.description: While Tecumseh was away recruiting, territorial governor William Henry Harrison marched on Prophetstown and fought the Prophet's followers, then burned the town. Tactically inconclusive, it convinced Americans the British in Canada were arming the confederacy.

events.label: William Henry Harrison
events.description: The territorial governor who fought at Tippecanoe and would ride the fame of that single battle, decades later, all the way to the presidency under the slogan 'Tippecanoe and Tyler too.'

events.label: Declaration of War on Britain
events.description: A divided Congress declared war on Britain by an unusually close vote, often described as the closest margin on any American declaration of a major war, the commercial Northeast mostly opposing the 'maritime rights' war while the agrarian West and South supported it.

events.label: The failed Canada invasions
events.description: The opening strategy to conquer Canada, which the War Hawks had promised would be easy, was a fiasco: bungled invasions collapsed, an American army surrendered Detroit without much of a fight, and Canada was never conquered.

events.label: The burning of Washington
events.description: A British force scattered American militia at Bladensburg, marched unopposed into the capital, ate the dinner laid out in the President's House, and burned it and the Capitol. Dolley Madison had a portrait of Washington cut from its frame and carried out.

events.label: Battle of Lake Erie
events.description: Oliver Hazard Perry, not yet thirty, built and manned a fleet from scratch on the frontier lake and captured an entire British squadron, reporting it in the line 'We have met the enemy and they are ours.'

events.label: Battle of the Thames
events.description: Control of Lake Erie let Harrison invade Canada from the west and win this battle, in which Tecumseh was killed, shattering the pan-Indian confederacy and ending the prospect of an organized Native barrier east of the Mississippi.

events.label: The Creek War
events.description: A civil war within the Creek nation between an accommodationist faction and the militant Red Sticks, which American forces entered on the anti-Red Stick side.

events.label: Battle of Horseshoe Bend
events.description: Frontier militia under Andrew Jackson crushed the Red Sticks, then imposed a treaty stripping the Creeks, allies and enemies alike, of a vast territory and opening the future cotton lands of Alabama and Mississippi.

events.label: Battle of Plattsburgh
events.description: An American naval victory on Lake Champlain stopped Britain's main invasion from Canada cold and convinced the British command the war was not worth the cost.

events.label: Battle of New Orleans
events.description: A large British army assaulting Andrew Jackson's fortified position was shattered, suffering roughly two thousand casualties against a few dozen American, fought two weeks after the Treaty of Ghent had already ended the war. It manufactured a national hero and a myth.

events.label: Andrew Jackson
events.description: A Tennessee planter and general, 'Old Hickory,' the man who beat the British at New Orleans and broke the Creeks; his entire later political rise, the populist tide that ends this book and dominates the next, runs straight out of New Orleans.

events.label: Hartford Convention
events.description: New England Federalist delegates met secretly and flirted with nullification and even leaving the Union. Its demands arrived in Washington alongside news of the Ghent peace and New Orleans victory, making it look like treason and effectively killing the Federalist Party.

events.label: The Era of Good Feelings
events.description: With the Federalists dead, the Democratic-Republicans had the field to themselves and James Monroe won essentially unopposed. The name is half-true and half-irony: confident nationalist calm over an unresolved, loading slavery question.

events.label: James Monroe elected President
events.description: A Virginian of the founding generation, a Revolutionary officer and Jefferson's protégé, Monroe was the last of the Virginia founders to hold the office, winning in 1816 and again in 1820 essentially unopposed.

events.label: Clay's American System
events.description: Henry Clay packaged a deliberately interlocking mechanism: a protective tariff to shield Northern industry and raise money, a national bank for stable currency, and federally funded internal improvements. It was Hamilton's vision resurrected by Hamilton's old enemies.

events.label: Second Bank of the United States
events.description: Chartered because the War of 1812's finances had been a catastrophe without a national bank, the same Jeffersonian Republicans who had let the first Bank die in 1811 now reversed themselves and re-created the institution.

events.label: McCulloch v. Maryland
events.description: Marshall's ruling held Congress could charter a bank under the 'necessary and proper' clause (the doctrine of implied powers, Hamilton's old argument) and that 'the power to tax involves the power to destroy,' so no state could tax a federal instrument.

events.label: Gibbons v. Ogden
events.description: The Marshall Court ruled the federal power over interstate commerce was broad and overrode conflicting state grants, striking down a state-granted steamboat monopoly and clearing the inland waters for open competition.

events.label: The Monroe Doctrine
events.description: Monroe declared the American continents closed to new European colonization and warned the United States would treat European recolonization as hostile. In 1823 it was a bold statement riding on the British Navy's power; it became a real pillar only decades later.

events.label: Panic of 1819
events.description: The young republic's first great boom-and-bust crisis: easy credit fueled a speculative land boom, the Bank abruptly tightened credit, the bubble burst, farms were foreclosed by the thousands, and a deep popular suspicion of banks set in.

events.label: The Missouri Crisis
events.description: Missouri applied for statehood as a slave state; a New York congressman's amendment to end slavery there passed the House on a stark sectional vote, and the country suddenly saw its deepest fault line ran not between parties but between North and South.

events.label: Missouri Compromise
events.description: Engineered largely by Henry Clay: Missouri admitted as a slave state, Maine as a free state to keep the Senate balanced, and slavery forbidden forever north of latitude 36°30′ in the rest of the Louisiana territory. It postponed the explosion for over thirty years.

events.label: Jefferson's 'fire bell in the night' letter
events.description: In a private letter to John Holmes, Jefferson wrote that the Missouri controversy 'like a fire bell in the night, awakened and filled me with terror. I considered it at once as the knell of the Union.' It is the pivot of the whole chain.

events.label: Election of 1824
events.description: Four candidates all calling themselves Republicans split the field; Jackson won the most popular and electoral votes but not a majority, throwing the election into the House.

events.label: The 'corrupt bargain'
events.description: Clay threw his support to John Quincy Adams, who was elected and then made Clay his Secretary of State. Jackson's supporters called it a backroom theft of an election; no documentary proof of a quid pro quo ever surfaced, but it handed Jackson a four-year grievance and a mass movement.

events.label: John Quincy Adams presidency
events.description: Son of the second president, a brilliant, cold, experienced New England diplomat, his single term was a frustrated failure: an ambitious nationalist program rejected by a hostile Congress and an anti-elite country.

events.label: Deaths of Adams and Jefferson
events.description: On July 4, 1826, the fiftieth anniversary to the day of the Declaration, Thomas Jefferson and John Adams both died within hours of each other. Adams's last words were that 'Thomas Jefferson survives,' not knowing Jefferson had died hours earlier.

events.label: Election of 1828
events.description: Andrew Jackson swept into the presidency on a wave of expanded white-male suffrage, mass political organization, and the claim that government should answer to the common man rather than a gentlemanly elite, the threshold of mass democracy.

events.label: Universal white male suffrage spreads
events.description: State after state dropped the property requirement to vote until something close to universal white male suffrage was the norm, a broader popular franchise than any other large state on earth, even as it pointedly excluded every woman and almost every Black American.

events.label: The First Great Awakening's legacy
events.description: A wave of fiery emotional preaching associated with the traveling English evangelist George Whitefield crossed the colonies in the 1740s and then faded; the Second Great Awakening was its much larger successor.

events.label: Richard Allen
events.description: A formerly enslaved Methodist preacher in Philadelphia, pulled up from his knees and ordered to a segregated gallery while praying, Allen walked out and founded the African Methodist Episcopal Church, for two centuries one of the most important institutions in Black American life.

events.label: John Adams, first minister to Britain
events.description: Sent to London as the first United States minister, Adams was received coldly and could get no concessions because everyone knew Congress could not make the states honor a treaty anyway, a humiliation that exposed the Confederation's weakness.

events.label: Benjamin Franklin in Paris
events.description: The printer, scientist, and elder statesman, in his late seventies, America's celebrated minister in Paris and one of the three negotiators who quietly disobeyed Congress to win a better treaty alone than France would have allowed.

events.label: The republican gamble
events.description: Every large republic in recorded history had rotted into tyranny, faction, or civil war, and the accepted theory, associated with Montesquieu, held that a republic could only survive in a small territory. A continental republic was, by the best political science of the day, a contradiction in terms.

events.label: Judicial review established
events.description: When an ordinary law conflicts with the Constitution, the Constitution wins and the courts must say so. Reasoned into existence by Marshall in a minor patronage dispute, it was close to unique in the world: Britain ran on parliamentary supremacy and no court could void an Act of Parliament.

events.label: Salutary neglect ends
events.description: For most of the colonial period Britain governed loosely, each colony taxing itself through its own assembly. That ended after 1763, when Britain, deep in debt from the Seven Years' War, decided the colonies should help pay for their own defense.

events.label: The French and Indian War
events.description: Britain's enormous, expensive war against France for control of North America, known in America as the French and Indian War. Its debts drove the British government to tax the colonies and started the imperial crisis.

events.label: Townshend duties
events.description: Parliament's taxes on imported goods like glass, paint, and tea, part of the series of post-1763 levies the colonists resisted on the constitutional principle, not the cost.

events.label: Puritan New England
events.description: New England was settled originally by strict Protestants who left England to build communities run on a severe Calvinist religion in which church membership, public morals, and town government were tightly bound together, the world the Second Great Awakening would later overturn.

events.label: The Southern plantation economy
events.description: The southern colonies were dominated by large agricultural estates growing tobacco and rice for export and running on the labor of enslaved Africans and their American-born descendants, an economy that the cotton revolution would massively expand.

events.label: The Iroquois Confederacy
events.description: Also called the Haudenosaunee, a league of six Native nations in what is now upstate New York that had governed itself for centuries by a consensus council, one of the oldest and most sophisticated political unions on the continent, shattered when the Revolution split its members.

events.label: The treaty power justifies Louisiana
events.description: The Constitution's explicit grant to the president, with Senate approval, to make treaties with foreign nations. Jefferson argued buying Louisiana was simply a treaty with France, even though 'treaties' had never been imagined to mean swallowing half a continent.

events.label: The Bladensburg Races
events.description: A British force scattered a hastily assembled American militia just outside Washington in a rout so complete it was mockingly called 'the Bladensburg Races,' leaving the capital open to be burned.

events.label: Dolley Madison saves the Washington portrait
events.description: The president's wife stayed in the President's House until the British were nearly upon it and had a large full-length portrait of George Washington cut from its frame and carried out so the enemy could not parade it as a trophy.

events.label: Oliver Hazard Perry
events.description: A Navy officer not yet thirty who built and manned a fleet from scratch on a frontier lake and won the Battle of Lake Erie, scrawling the news in a field note whose opening line entered American memory.

events.label: Madison's presidency
events.description: Jefferson handed the foreign crisis to his closest ally, the chief architect of the Constitution himself, now operating the machine he had designed under an international crisis it had never been built for and drifting toward a war he did not want.

events.label: Prophetstown
events.description: The base on the Tippecanoe River from which Tecumseh and the Prophet built a pan-Indian confederacy and religious revival, the most serious organized challenge to American westward expansion the country had yet faced.

events.label: Fraudulent land-cession treaties
events.description: American officials found a few cooperative chiefs, often plied with goods or alcohol, and treated their signatures as binding whole peoples, even though land was held collectively and a sale needed broad consent the signers had no right to give.

events.label: William Marbury's undelivered commission
events.description: One midnight-judge commission, for a justice of the peace named William Marbury, was signed but not delivered before Jefferson took office; Jefferson's Secretary of State refused to deliver it, and Marbury sued, producing the case that invented judicial review.

events.label: Status quo ante bellum
events.description: The Treaty of Ghent ended the war on the basis of 'the state in which things were before the war': no territory changed hands and not one stated American grievance, including impressment, was even mentioned. 'Nothing changed' nonetheless changed everything in American self-perception.

events.label: The Red Sticks
events.description: The militant faction within the Creek nation, named for the red-painted war clubs that were their symbol, crushed by Andrew Jackson at Horseshoe Bend in a civil war inside the larger War of 1812.

events.label: William Crawford
events.description: Monroe's Treasury secretary and the early favorite of the party establishment in the 1824 race, partly disabled by a stroke during the campaign.

events.label: The state-bank boom
events.description: A swarm of loosely regulated locally chartered private banks issued their own paper money with little oversight, fueling the speculative land boom that burst in the Panic of 1819.

events.label: The Holy Alliance threat
events.description: The bloc of conservative monarchies (Russia, Prussia, Austria) that claimed a right to crush liberal revolutions in Europe and might extend that principle to help Spain reconquer its lost American colonies, the threat the Monroe Doctrine answered.

events.label: Latin American independence
events.description: By the early 1820s the Spanish empire in the Americas was collapsing and most of Latin America had won independence, much of it explicitly inspired by the American and French revolutions, with leaders like Simón Bolívar citing the United States as proof a colony could become a republic.

events.label: The loyal opposition
events.description: The idea that you can lose an election, oppose the government with everything you have, and still be a legitimate participant rather than a traitor to be crushed. The American system acquired it, in practice, in 1801, the single thing most distinguishing a working republic from the failed ones.

events.label: The Shoshone
events.description: A Native people of the Rocky Mountain and Great Basin region; Sacagawea, born among them and captured as a child, used her ability to interpret and negotiate with mountain nations to keep the Lewis and Clark expedition alive.

events.label: Rochambeau's French army
events.description: The professional general commanding the roughly 5,000 French regulars sent to fight alongside the Americans, whose army joined Washington's to shut the land door at Yorktown.

events.label: Black soldiers in the Revolution
events.description: Both sides used the promise of freedom as a weapon; tens of thousands of enslaved Americans fled to British lines, the largest emancipation in North America before the Civil War, while Patriots more reluctantly enlisted Black soldiers with promises sometimes kept and often not.

story.title: The Early American Republic
story.subtitle: From a failed experiment to a continental nation, 1783–1815

paths.label: Building the Republic
paths.description: How a loose confederation of bickering states forged a constitution, built a government from scratch, and doubled the nation's size — all while arguing about whether they'd created a nation or a mistake.

nodes.narrative:
The Revolution is over. America has won its independence. And the country is falling apart.

1786. The Articles of Confederation have created a national government so weak it can't collect taxes, regulate trade, or raise an army. Each state prints its own currency. Interstate commerce is chaos. The national debt from the Revolution goes unpaid.

In western Massachusetts, the crisis hits home. Farmers who fought in the Revolution are drowning in debt. Courts are seizing their farms. The state legislature — dominated by Boston merchants — refuses to help. So Daniel Shays, a decorated Continental Army captain, does what Americans do when government fails them: he rebels.

Shays and 1,500 armed farmers shut down courthouses to stop foreclosures. When they march on the federal arsenal at Springfield in January 1787, a privately funded militia scatters them with cannon fire. Four rebels die. Shays flees to Vermont.

The rebellion is crushed, but the message is received. George Washington, watching from Mount Vernon, writes: 'There are combustibles in every State which a spark might set fire to.' If the federal government can't maintain order in Massachusetts, the entire experiment in self-governance is doomed.

Shays' Rebellion doesn't just expose the Articles' weakness — it provides the urgency that drives the Constitutional Convention. The founders need a crisis to justify starting over, and Daniel Shays has just given them one.

quiz.question: Why couldn't the federal government respond to Shays' Rebellion?
options: The Articles of Confederation gave it no power to raise an army or collect taxes
The Supreme Court ruled it unconstitutional
quiz.explanation: Under the Articles of Confederation, the federal government had no power to tax, raise troops, or intervene in state affairs.

quiz.question: What were the Massachusetts farmers rebelling against?
options: Courts seizing their farms for unpaid debts
quiz.explanation: Debt-ridden farmers were losing their property to court-ordered foreclosures, while the state legislature refused relief.

quiz.question: What was Daniel Shays's background before leading the rebellion?
options: A British loyalist who switched sides
A decorated captain in the Continental Army
quiz.explanation: Shays had served with distinction in the Revolution, fighting at Bunker Hill, Saratoga, and Stony Point.

quiz.question: What was the most significant consequence of Shays' Rebellion?
options: It ended debtor imprisonment in Massachusetts
It convinced Britain to attack again
It freed farmers from their debts
It created the urgency for the Constitutional Convention
quiz.explanation: The rebellion proved the Articles of Confederation couldn't maintain order, giving reformers the crisis they needed to push for a new constitution.

nodes.narrative:
Shays' Rebellion has proven that the Articles of Confederation are a dead letter. Now the question is whether the states will fix the problem — or whether the experiment ends here.

1787. Fifty-five delegates arrive in Philadelphia, ostensibly to revise the Articles. Behind the locked doors of the Pennsylvania State House, with windows nailed shut against eavesdroppers in the sweltering summer heat, they do something far more radical: they throw out the Articles entirely and write a new constitution from scratch.

The debates are brutal. Virginia's James Madison arrives with a detailed plan for a strong national government — representation by population. Small states like New Jersey revolt: they'll walk out before accepting a system where Virginia and Pennsylvania dominate. For weeks the convention teeters on collapse.

Roger Sherman of Connecticut brokers the Great Compromise: a House of Representatives apportioned by population and a Senate with two members per state. It saves the convention. But the ugliest bargain follows — the Three-Fifths Compromise counts each enslaved person as three-fifths of a free person for representation, giving slaveholding states outsized power.

Benjamin Franklin, 81 years old and so frail he's carried to sessions in a sedan chair, provides the closing wisdom. Asked what the convention has created, he replies: 'A republic, madam — if you can keep it.' The challenge of keeping it begins immediately.

quiz.question: What was the original stated purpose of the Constitutional Convention?
options: To revise the Articles of Confederation
quiz.explanation: The delegates were officially called to revise the Articles of Confederation — instead they scrapped them entirely.

quiz.question: What did the Great Compromise create?
options: A bicameral legislature with the House by population and equal state representation in the Senate
quiz.explanation: Roger Sherman's Great Compromise gave large states proportional representation in the House and small states equal votes in the Senate.

quiz.question: Why were the windows of the convention hall nailed shut?
options: To prevent eavesdroppers from hearing the secret debates
To keep the room warm in winter
quiz.explanation: The delegates agreed to strict secrecy, nailing windows shut despite the Philadelphia summer heat to prevent anyone from overhearing.

quiz.question: What was Benjamin Franklin's famous reply about what the convention had created?
options: 'A republic, madam — if you can keep it'
quiz.explanation: Franklin's quip to Elizabeth Willing Powel acknowledged that the republic's survival depended on its citizens, not just its Constitution.

nodes.narrative:
The convention has produced a document — but now it must survive the most bruising political fight in American history. Ratification requires nine of thirteen states, and the opposition is fierce.

The Anti-Federalists are formidable. Patrick Henry thunders that the Constitution is a blueprint for tyranny. George Mason, who attended the convention but refused to sign, warns that without a bill of rights, the new government will be indistinguishable from the one they overthrew. In New York, Governor George Clinton organizes opposition that threatens to keep the largest commercial state out of the union.

The Federalists fight back with the most influential series of political essays ever written. Alexander Hamilton, James Madison, and John Jay publish 85 Federalist Papers under the pseudonym 'Publius,' arguing that the Constitution's structure itself protects liberty. Hamilton writes 51 of them at a nearly superhuman pace.

The ratification battle is razor-close. In Massachusetts, it passes by 19 votes. In Virginia, by 10. In New York — Hamilton's home state — by just 3. On June 21, 1788, New Hampshire becomes the ninth state to ratify, and the Constitution takes effect.

It is a framework, not a finished product. The founders know this. They've built in an amendment process precisely because they know future generations will need to fix what they got wrong. The first ten amendments — the Bill of Rights — are already being demanded as the price of ratification.

quiz.question: How many of the 85 Federalist Papers did Alexander Hamilton write?
quiz.explanation: Hamilton wrote 51 of the 85 essays — an astonishing output that helped swing New York toward ratification.

quiz.question: Which state ratified by the narrowest margin?
quiz.explanation: New York ratified by a margin of only 3 votes — Hamilton's Federalist Papers and personal lobbying were crucial.

quiz.question: Why did George Mason refuse to sign the Constitution?
options: He thought it was too weak
It lacked a bill of rights protecting individual liberties
quiz.explanation: Mason insisted that without explicit protections for individual rights, the new government would become tyrannical.

quiz.question: How many states needed to ratify for the Constitution to take effect?
quiz.explanation: Nine of the thirteen states needed to ratify. New Hampshire became the ninth on June 21, 1788.

nodes.narrative:
The Constitution exists on paper — now someone must bring it to life. The nation turns to the one man everyone trusts.

April 30, 1789. George Washington takes the oath of office on the balcony of Federal Hall in New York City. He is the unanimous choice of the Electoral College — the only president in American history to achieve this. His hands are shaking. He knows that every single thing he does will set a precedent.

And he is right. Washington insists on being called 'Mr. President,' rejecting grander titles like 'His Highness' or 'Your Excellency.' He assembles the first Cabinet — Jefferson at State, Hamilton at Treasury, Knox at War. He establishes that the president can remove appointees without Senate approval. He issues the first presidential veto.

But Washington's greatest challenge is holding the country together as Hamilton and Jefferson tear it apart. Hamilton wants a powerful central government, a national bank, and an industrial economy. Jefferson wants states' rights, agrarian democracy, and strict limits on federal power. Their feud creates the first political parties — Hamilton's Federalists and Jefferson's Democratic-Republicans.

Washington tries to stand above the fray, but the divisions cut deep. By his second term, he's exhausted. In 1796, he voluntarily steps down — the most consequential decision of his presidency. The peaceful transfer of power to John Adams, unbroken for over two centuries, begins here.

quiz.question: Why is Washington's inauguration unique in American history?
options: He was unanimously elected by the Electoral College
quiz.explanation: Washington is the only president ever elected unanimously by the Electoral College — both times he ran.

quiz.question: What philosophical divide shaped Washington's presidency?
options: Hamilton's strong federal government vs. Jefferson's states' rights
quiz.explanation: The Hamilton-Jefferson rivalry over the scope of federal power created the first political parties and defined American politics.

quiz.question: What title did Washington choose for the president?
quiz.explanation: Washington rejected grander titles, establishing the simple 'Mr. President' that endures today.

quiz.question: What was the most important precedent Washington set by retiring?
options: Peaceful transfer of power and a two-term tradition
That the vice president cannot succeed the president
That Cabinet members must resign with the president
quiz.explanation: Washington's voluntary retirement established the tradition of peaceful power transfer and the two-term limit later codified in the 22nd Amendment.

nodes.narrative:
Washington has set the tone for the presidency, but the nation he leads is drowning in debt — and his brash young Treasury Secretary has a plan that will either rescue or wreck the republic.

1790. The United States owes $75 million from the Revolution. States are printing worthless currency. Foreign creditors are walking away. Alexander Hamilton, the 35-year-old Treasury Secretary — an immigrant orphan from the Caribbean who talked his way into Washington's inner circle — proposes something radical.

The federal government will assume all state war debts. It will charter a national bank. It will impose excise taxes to pay for everything. The idea is breathtaking in its ambition: bind the states together through shared financial obligation, and make the wealthy invested in the government's success by tying their fortunes to federal bonds.

Jefferson and Madison are horrified. They see Hamilton building an American aristocracy of bankers and merchants. The plan, they argue, rewards Northern speculators who bought up depreciated war bonds from soldiers for pennies on the dollar. The deadlock threatens to shatter the government in its infancy.

The breakthrough happens over dinner. Jefferson invites Hamilton and Madison to his home. Hamilton agrees to move the national capital from New York to a site on the Potomac River — closer to the agrarian South. In exchange, Jefferson and Madison support Hamilton's financial plan.

It works. American credit is restored almost overnight. European investors pour money into federal bonds. The economy stabilizes. But the philosophical divide — big government vs. small, commerce vs. agriculture, Hamilton vs. Jefferson — will define American politics for the next 250 years.

quiz.question: What deal resolved the deadlock over Hamilton's financial plan?
options: Hamilton agreed to lower taxes on Southern farmers
Jefferson agreed to support a national army
Hamilton agreed to move the capital to the Potomac in exchange for support of his plan
Madison agreed to dissolve the Democratic-Republican Party
quiz.explanation: At a famous dinner, Hamilton traded the capital's location for Jefferson and Madison's support of his financial system.

quiz.question: Why did Jefferson oppose Hamilton's financial plan?
options: He thought it would bankrupt the country
He wanted to keep the capital in New York
He believed it created an aristocracy of bankers that favored Northern speculators over farmers
He wanted to default on the war debts
quiz.explanation: Jefferson saw Hamilton's system as enriching Northern merchants and speculators at the expense of Southern agrarian interests.

quiz.question: What was Hamilton's background before becoming Treasury Secretary?
options: An immigrant orphan from the Caribbean
quiz.explanation: Hamilton was born in the Caribbean, orphaned as a child, and rose through sheer brilliance and force of will.

quiz.question: How did Hamilton's plan bind the states together?
options: By requiring states to share militias
By having the federal government assume all state war debts
quiz.explanation: Federal assumption of state debts meant every state was invested in the national government's financial success.

nodes.narrative:
Hamilton's financial system has stabilized the economy, but a deeper question remains: what protections do individual citizens have against the very government they have just empowered?

1791. The Constitution has been in effect for barely two years, and already the Anti-Federalists' warnings feel prophetic. Patrick Henry thunders that the document is a recipe for tyranny — a powerful central government with no explicit guarantee of individual rights. George Mason, who helped write the Constitution but refused to sign it, warns that without protections, the new government will become the thing they fought a revolution to escape.

James Madison initially dismisses the idea of a bill of rights. The structure of government itself, he argues, is the best protection for liberty. But the ratification fight changes his mind. Several states ratify only on the explicit promise that amendments will follow. Madison, now a Virginia congressman, drafts seventeen amendments drawn from state constitutions, English common law, and Enlightenment philosophy.

Congress whittles the seventeen down to twelve and sends them to the states. Ten are ratified. They guarantee freedoms that Americans will argue over for centuries: speech, religion, press, assembly, the right to bear arms, protection against unreasonable searches, the right to a jury trial, and protection against cruel and unusual punishment.

The Bill of Rights transforms the Constitution from a blueprint for government into a shield for the governed. Jefferson writes from Paris: 'A bill of rights is what the people are entitled to against every government on earth.' The American experiment now has both an engine and a brake.

quiz.question: Why did James Madison initially oppose a bill of rights?
options: He believed the Constitution's structure itself protected liberty
He thought it would make the government too weak
He wanted to wait for a second constitutional convention
He believed rights were already protected by state laws
quiz.explanation: Madison argued that the separation of powers and checks and balances built into the Constitution were sufficient protection.

quiz.question: How many amendments did Madison originally propose?
quiz.explanation: Madison drafted 17 amendments. Congress reduced them to 12, and the states ratified 10 — the Bill of Rights.

quiz.question: Which of the following is NOT protected by the First Amendment?
quiz.explanation: The right to bear arms is protected by the Second Amendment, not the First.

quiz.question: What role did Anti-Federalist opposition play in creating the Bill of Rights?
options: They blocked ratification until amendments were promised
Several states ratified only on the condition that a bill of rights would follow
They had no influence — Madison always planned the amendments
quiz.explanation: Anti-Federalist pressure forced the promise of amendments as a condition of ratification in several key states.

nodes.narrative:
The Bill of Rights has secured individual liberty and the Constitution has survived its first turbulent decade. Now Jefferson — the champion of limited government — faces a temptation that will force him to abandon his own principles.

1803. Napoleon Bonaparte offers to sell the entire Louisiana Territory — 828,000 square miles stretching from the Mississippi River to the Rocky Mountains — for $15 million. That works out to about three cents an acre. It is the greatest real estate bargain in human history.

The problem: nothing in the Constitution explicitly authorizes the president to purchase foreign territory. Jefferson, who spent years attacking Hamilton for reading powers into the Constitution that weren't there, must now do exactly the same thing — or pass up the deal of the millennium.

He wrestles with it. He drafts a constitutional amendment. Then he throws the draft away. Napoleon might change his mind at any moment. Jefferson swallows his principles and signs the deal.

Why is Napoleon selling? Because the Haitian Revolution has destroyed his Caribbean empire. He's lost 50,000 soldiers trying to retake the island. He needs cash for his European wars. And he'd rather strengthen America than let Britain seize the territory.

The Louisiana Purchase doubles the size of the United States overnight. It secures control of the Mississippi River and the port of New Orleans, lifelines for western farmers. It opens the way for Lewis and Clark to map a route to the Pacific. And it sets the precedent that the federal government can act boldly when opportunity demands — even if the Constitution doesn't explicitly say so.

Jefferson's three-cent acres will become all or part of fifteen future states.

quiz.question: Why did Jefferson hesitate to make the Louisiana Purchase?
options: He thought the price was too high
The Constitution didn't explicitly authorize the president to buy foreign territory
Congress had already rejected the idea
quiz.explanation: Jefferson, a strict constructionist, agonized over acting without explicit constitutional authority — but ultimately chose pragmatism.

quiz.question: What event in the Caribbean directly led Napoleon to sell Louisiana?
options: The Haitian Revolution destroyed his Caribbean empire
quiz.explanation: Napoleon lost 50,000 soldiers trying to retake Haiti, abandoning his dreams of a Western Hemisphere empire.

quiz.question: What critical waterway did the Purchase secure for American farmers?
options: The Mississippi River and the port of New Orleans
quiz.explanation: Control of the Mississippi and New Orleans was vital — western farmers depended on the river to ship their goods to market.

quiz.question: How many future states would be carved from the Louisiana Territory?
quiz.explanation: All or part of 15 future states were carved from the 828,000 square miles of the Louisiana Purchase.

paths.label: The First Crises
paths.description: A tax revolt, an undeclared naval war, laws criminalizing free speech, a Supreme Court power grab, pirates on the Barbary Coast, and the British burning the White House. Follow the crises that tested whether the new republic could survive its own contradictions.

nodes.narrative:
Hamilton's financial system has saved the economy — but the farmers on the western frontier are paying the price, and they're reaching for their muskets.

1794. Hamilton's excise tax on whiskey hits western Pennsylvania like a sledgehammer. For frontier farmers, whiskey isn't a luxury — it's currency. Their grain is too bulky to ship across the mountains, so they distill it. A horse can carry four bushels of grain or 24 bushels' worth of whiskey. The tax falls hardest on the people least able to pay it.

Resistance begins with petitions and escalates to violence. Tax collectors are tarred and feathered. A federal marshal serving writs is attacked. When 500 armed rebels march on the home of the regional tax inspector and burn it to the ground, the crisis reaches the president's desk.
