Washington's response is extraordinary. He personally leads 13,000 militia — larger than any army he commanded during the Revolution — across the Appalachians to crush the rebellion. It is the first and only time a sitting president has commanded troops in the field.

The rebels melt away. There is no battle. Two men are convicted of treason; Washington pardons both. The point has been made: the federal government under the Constitution, unlike under the Articles, can enforce its laws. The question Shays' Rebellion raised has been answered.

But the political cost is steep. Western farmers flock to Jefferson's Democratic-Republicans, deepening the partisan divide that Hamilton and Jefferson have already carved into American politics.

quiz.question: Why was whiskey so important to western frontier farmers?
options: It served as currency because grain was too bulky to ship across the mountains
British merchants demanded it as payment
quiz.explanation: Distilling grain into whiskey made it compact and valuable enough to transport over the Appalachians — it was essentially liquid currency.

quiz.question: What made Washington's response to the Whiskey Rebellion historically unique?
options: He negotiated a compromise with the rebels
He declared martial law for the first time
He personally led 13,000 troops — the only sitting president to command in the field
He asked Congress to repeal the tax
quiz.explanation: Washington personally marched at the head of an army larger than anything he'd commanded in the Revolution.

quiz.question: How did the Whiskey Rebellion differ from Shays' Rebellion in outcome?
options: This time the federal government had the power to enforce its laws
quiz.explanation: Under the Constitution, unlike under the Articles of Confederation, the federal government could raise an army and enforce its laws.

quiz.question: What happened to the two men convicted of treason?
quiz.explanation: Washington pardoned both convicted rebels — demonstrating federal power while showing mercy.

nodes.narrative:
Washington has retired, and the man who replaces him inherits a government tearing itself apart — with a foreign crisis on top.

1797. John Adams takes office as the second president, and the situation is dire. France, furious that the United States signed a trade treaty with Britain, is seizing American ships. In the 'XYZ Affair,' French diplomats demand a $250,000 bribe and a $10 million loan before they'll even negotiate. When the dispatches are published, Americans are enraged. 'Millions for defense, but not one cent for tribute!' becomes the rallying cry.

Hamilton and the Federalists are itching for war. They see a chance to build a professional army, expand federal power, and crush Jefferson's pro-French Democratic-Republicans. Adams has a different instinct. He sends a new diplomatic mission to Paris, and in 1800 the Convention of Mortefontaine ends the Quasi-War without a single land battle.

Adams's decision for peace is courageous — and politically fatal. His own party turns against him. Hamilton, who privately despises Adams, works behind the scenes to undermine his reelection. The Federalists split.

But Adams's greatest crisis is still to come. In the heat of the Quasi-War, he signs the Alien and Sedition Acts — laws that will test whether the First Amendment means anything at all. Adams later calls his decision to avoid full-scale war 'the most disinterested action of my life.' History agrees. But the Sedition Act will haunt his legacy forever.

quiz.question: What was the XYZ Affair?
options: French diplomats demanded bribes before they would negotiate with American envoys
A British plot to assassinate Adams
quiz.explanation: French agents (called X, Y, and Z in the published dispatches) demanded $250,000 in bribes and a $10 million loan.

quiz.question: Why was Adams's decision to pursue peace with France politically costly?
options: The public wanted war with France
His own Federalist Party, led by Hamilton, wanted war and turned against him
quiz.explanation: Hamilton and war-hawk Federalists saw the crisis as an opportunity to expand federal power, and they turned on Adams when he chose diplomacy.

quiz.question: What did Adams later call his decision to avoid war with France?
options: 'The most disinterested action of my life'
quiz.explanation: Adams knew peace would cost him reelection but believed it was right — and called it the most selfless act of his career.

quiz.question: How did the Quasi-War with France end?
options: French invasion of the United States
The Convention of Mortefontaine ended it diplomatically in 1800
quiz.explanation: Adams sent diplomats to Paris, and the Convention of Mortefontaine ended hostilities without a full-scale war.

nodes.narrative:
Adams has averted war with France, but in the process he has signed laws that threaten the very freedoms the Revolution was fought to protect.

1798. With anti-French hysteria at a fever pitch, the Federalist-controlled Congress passes four laws known collectively as the Alien and Sedition Acts. The Alien Acts give the president power to deport any non-citizen deemed 'dangerous.' The Naturalization Act extends the residency requirement for citizenship from five to fourteen years — conveniently targeting Irish and French immigrants who tend to vote for Jefferson.

But the Sedition Act is the bombshell. It criminalizes 'false, scandalous, and malicious' statements about the president or Congress. In plain English: criticize the government and go to jail.

The crackdown is immediate. Twenty-five people are arrested. Newspaper editors are prosecuted for writing op-eds. Congressman Matthew Lyon of Vermont is jailed for writing a letter that accused Adams of 'ridiculous pomp.' He wins reelection from his cell.

Jefferson and Madison fight back in secret. They draft the Kentucky and Virginia Resolutions, arguing that states can nullify unconstitutional federal laws. It's a dangerous doctrine — one that will resurface before the Civil War — but in 1798 it's the only weapon they have.

The Sedition Act expires on the last day of Adams's presidency — March 3, 1801 — by design. The Federalists made sure it couldn't be used against them. But the damage is done. Adams loses the 1800 election. The Alien and Sedition Acts become a cautionary tale about what happens when fear overrides the Bill of Rights.

quiz.question: What did the Sedition Act make illegal?
options: 'False, scandalous, and malicious' criticism of the president or Congress
quiz.explanation: The Sedition Act criminalized criticism of the government — a direct assault on First Amendment protections.

quiz.question: What happened to Congressman Matthew Lyon under the Sedition Act?
options: He was jailed for criticizing Adams — and won reelection from his cell
quiz.explanation: Lyon was convicted for writing a letter critical of Adams's 'ridiculous pomp' and served four months in jail while his constituents reelected him.

quiz.question: Why did the Sedition Act expire on March 3, 1801?
options: The Supreme Court struck it down
The Federalists designed it to expire before it could be used against them if they lost power
quiz.explanation: The Federalists wrote the expiration date into the law so it couldn't be turned against them if they lost the next election.

quiz.question: How did Jefferson and Madison respond to the Alien and Sedition Acts?
options: They filed a lawsuit in the Supreme Court
They secretly drafted the Kentucky and Virginia Resolutions arguing states could nullify unconstitutional laws
They supported the acts publicly but opposed them privately
quiz.explanation: Jefferson and Madison anonymously authored the Kentucky and Virginia Resolutions, introducing the controversial doctrine of state nullification.

nodes.narrative:
The Sedition Act is dead, Jefferson is president, and now the Supreme Court makes its most audacious power grab in American history — and nobody can stop it.

1803. The case seems minor. William Marbury, a Federalist appointee, wants his commission as a justice of the peace. Adams signed it, but Jefferson's Secretary of State — James Madison — refuses to deliver it. Marbury sues.

Chief Justice John Marshall faces an impossible dilemma. If he orders Madison to deliver the commission, Jefferson will simply ignore the order, proving the Court is powerless. If he sides with Madison, the Federalist judiciary surrenders to the Democratic-Republicans.

Marshall's solution is a masterpiece of political judo. He rules that Marbury has a right to his commission — but then declares that the law Marbury used to bring the case is itself unconstitutional. The Supreme Court, Marshall announces, has the power to strike down any law that violates the Constitution.

Jefferson can't object. He won the case. Marbury doesn't get his commission. But in losing, Marshall has claimed the most important power any court in the world has ever held: judicial review.

No one fully grasps the implications in 1803. But Marshall has just made the Supreme Court a co-equal branch of government. Every major constitutional battle for the next two centuries — from Dred Scott to Brown v. Board to Roe v. Wade — flows from this moment. The judiciary is no longer the 'least dangerous branch.' It is the final arbiter of what the Constitution means.

quiz.question: What power did Marbury v. Madison establish for the Supreme Court?
options: The power to impeach the president
Judicial review — the power to strike down unconstitutional laws
The power to appoint federal judges
quiz.explanation: Marshall established that the Supreme Court has the final say on whether a law violates the Constitution.

quiz.question: Why was Marshall's ruling considered political genius?
options: He sided with both parties simultaneously
He gave Jefferson the outcome he wanted while claiming a far greater power for the Court
He avoided making any ruling at all
He delayed the case until Adams could intervene
quiz.explanation: By ruling against Marbury on the surface, Marshall prevented Jefferson from defying the Court — while establishing judicial review.

quiz.question: What was the original dispute in the case?
options: Whether the president could veto Supreme Court decisions
Whether William Marbury should receive his judicial commission
Whether Congress could create new courts
quiz.explanation: Marbury sued because Jefferson's Secretary of State refused to deliver his commission as justice of the peace.

quiz.question: Alexander Hamilton called the judiciary the 'least dangerous branch.' How did Marshall change that?
options: By creating a judicial police force
By making the Court a co-equal branch with the power to invalidate laws
By expanding the number of justices
By giving judges lifetime military commands
quiz.explanation: Judicial review transformed the Court from a passive interpreter into the final arbiter of constitutional meaning.

nodes.narrative:
Marshall has empowered the judiciary at home, but thousands of miles away, the young republic faces a very different kind of test — pirates.

1801. For decades, the Barbary States of North Africa — Tripoli, Algiers, Tunis, and Morocco — have demanded tribute from any nation whose ships sail the Mediterranean. European powers pay up. Under the Articles of Confederation, the United States paid too — over $1 million, about one-tenth of the entire federal budget.

Jefferson refuses to keep paying. When the Pasha of Tripoli declares war by chopping down the American flagpole at his palace, Jefferson sends the Navy.

The war is a proving ground for the young American military. In 1803, the USS Philadelphia runs aground in Tripoli's harbor and its crew is captured. Lieutenant Stephen Decatur leads a raiding party into the harbor at night, boards the ship, and burns it to prevent the enemy from using it. British Admiral Horatio Nelson calls it 'the most bold and daring act of the age.'

The war drags on until 1805, when a combined naval blockade and an overland assault by a tiny force of Marines and mercenaries force Tripoli to terms. The Marines' march across the Libyan desert — 'to the shores of Tripoli' — enters the Marine Corps hymn.

The First Barbary War is small, but its significance is large. The United States has projected military power overseas for the first time, refused to pay tribute to extortionists, and proven that the new navy can fight. For a country less than twenty years old, it's a statement.

quiz.question: How did the Pasha of Tripoli declare war on the United States?
options: By sending a formal declaration to Congress
By chopping down the American flagpole at his palace
quiz.explanation: In the Barbary tradition, cutting down a nation's flagpole was a declaration of war.

quiz.question: What did Lieutenant Stephen Decatur do to the captured USS Philadelphia?
options: Led a night raid to board and burn it so the enemy couldn't use it
Launched a full naval assault to recapture it
quiz.explanation: Decatur's night raid to burn the Philadelphia was called 'the most bold and daring act of the age' by Admiral Nelson.

quiz.question: What phrase in the Marine Corps hymn comes from the Barbary Wars?
options: 'In the air, on land, and sea'
'First to fight for right and freedom'
quiz.explanation: The Marines' march across the Libyan desert to attack Tripoli gave the hymn its famous second line.

quiz.question: Why was the First Barbary War significant for the young republic?
options: It ended European colonialism in Africa
It was the first time the U.S. projected military power overseas
quiz.explanation: The Barbary War proved the young nation could project military force internationally and refuse to pay tribute.

nodes.narrative:
The republic has faced down pirates and internal rebellions. Now it must survive a rematch with the most powerful empire on earth.

1812. Britain is at war with Napoleon, and it's squeezing the United States in the process. The Royal Navy stops American ships and 'impresses' — kidnaps — American sailors, forcing them to serve on British warships. Britain supplies weapons to Native American nations fighting American expansion. Trade restrictions strangle American commerce.

President James Madison, pressured by 'War Hawks' in Congress like Henry Clay and John C. Calhoun, asks for a declaration of war. The vote is the closest war vote in American history — 79 to 49 in the House.

The war goes badly at first. An American invasion of Canada fails spectacularly. The British burn Washington, D.C., in August 1814 — the president and first lady flee the capital as the White House, the Capitol, and the Treasury go up in flames. Dolley Madison saves Gilbert Stuart's portrait of George Washington by cutting it from its frame.

But the Americans rally. Fort McHenry withstands a 25-hour British bombardment, inspiring Francis Scott Key to write 'The Star-Spangled Banner.' Andrew Jackson's ragtag army destroys a professional British force at the Battle of New Orleans in January 1815 — two weeks after the Treaty of Ghent has already ended the war, though neither side knows it yet.

The War of 1812 ends in a draw on paper. But it ends something larger: the question of whether the American republic can survive. No European power will seriously threaten American sovereignty again. The Era of Good Feelings begins.

quiz.question: What practice of the British Navy was a major cause of the War of 1812?
options: Impressing (kidnapping) American sailors to serve on British warships
Refusing to trade with American merchants
quiz.explanation: Impressment — the forcible conscription of American sailors — was a direct assault on American sovereignty and a key cause of the war.

quiz.question: What happened when the British attacked Washington, D.C.?
options: They burned the White House, Capitol, and Treasury while the president fled
They occupied the city for a year
quiz.explanation: The British burned major government buildings in August 1814 — one of the most humiliating moments in American history.

quiz.question: What inspired Francis Scott Key to write 'The Star-Spangled Banner'?
options: Fort McHenry withstanding a 25-hour British bombardment
The signing of the Treaty of Ghent
quiz.explanation: Key watched the bombardment from a British ship and saw the American flag still flying at dawn — inspiring the national anthem.

quiz.question: Why was the Battle of New Orleans ironic?
options: It was fought between two American generals
It was fought two weeks after the peace treaty had already been signed
The British won but didn't realize it
It was fought on French territory
quiz.explanation: The Treaty of Ghent was signed on December 24, 1814, but news traveled so slowly that Jackson fought — and won — on January 8, 1815.

paths.label: Inventors & Explorers
paths.description: The cotton gin, interchangeable parts, a steamboat that changed everything, and an expedition to the edge of the known world. Follow the inventors and explorers who transformed a coastal republic into a continental power.

nodes.narrative:
The republic is barely a decade old, and a Yale graduate tinkering on a Georgia plantation is about to reshape the American economy — in ways he never intended.

1794. Eli Whitney has a problem he thinks he can solve. Short-staple cotton grows well across the South, but its sticky green seeds are almost impossible to remove by hand. A worker can clean about one pound per day. It takes all day. The labor makes cotton barely profitable.

Whitney builds a simple machine: a wooden cylinder with wire hooks that pull cotton fiber through a mesh screen, leaving the seeds behind. His cotton gin — short for 'engine' — can clean 50 pounds of cotton in a single day. He patents it in 1794.

The effect is instantaneous and catastrophic. Cotton becomes wildly profitable overnight. Southern planters tear out their tobacco and indigo fields and plant cotton horizon to horizon. But who will pick it? The gin cleans cotton, but it doesn't harvest it. That still requires backbreaking hand labor.

The demand for enslaved workers explodes. In 1790, the South has about 700,000 enslaved people and produces 3,000 bales of cotton. By 1860: nearly 4 million enslaved people and 4.5 million bales — three-quarters of the world's supply. Cotton becomes America's most valuable export. Northern mills spin it. New York banks finance it. British factories weave it.

Whitney invents a machine to save labor. Instead, he creates the economic engine that makes slavery the foundation of the American economy. It's the most consequential unintended consequence in American history.

quiz.question: What problem did the cotton gin solve?
options: Removing sticky seeds from short-staple cotton fiber
quiz.explanation: The gin's wire hooks pulled cotton fiber through a mesh screen, separating it from the seeds that made hand-cleaning so tedious.

quiz.question: How did the cotton gin affect the institution of slavery?
options: It reduced the need for enslaved labor
It had no effect on slavery
It massively increased demand for enslaved labor to harvest the now-profitable crop
quiz.explanation: The gin made cotton hugely profitable but didn't mechanize harvesting — driving demand for enslaved workers from 700,000 to nearly 4 million.

quiz.question: What is 'gin' short for?
quiz.explanation: Cotton gin is short for 'cotton engine' — Whitney's simple but revolutionary machine.

quiz.question: What share of the world's cotton did the American South produce by 1860?
quiz.explanation: By 1860, the South produced about three-quarters of the world's cotton supply — 4.5 million bales annually.

nodes.narrative:
Whitney's cotton gin has transformed the South. Now he's about to plant the seed of an idea that will transform the North — even if his own demonstration is a bit of a con.

1801. Eli Whitney is broke. His cotton gin patent has been pirated by every planter in the South, and he's spent years in fruitless lawsuits. He needs a new revenue stream. He promises the federal government 10,000 muskets in two years — an insane commitment for a man with no factory and no experience making weapons.

Whitney's revolutionary pitch: instead of having a master craftsman hand-file each musket as a unique work of art, he'll make standardized parts that any worker can assemble. A broken lock can be replaced with any other lock. A damaged barrel swaps out in minutes. No skilled gunsmiths needed.

In January 1801, Whitney demonstrates before President Adams and members of Congress. He lays out piles of musket parts, randomly selects components, and assembles working firearms. The audience is astonished.

Historians now believe the demonstration was partly staged — Whitney may have pre-fitted the parts. He delivers the 10,000 muskets eight years late. But the idea is genuine, and it catches fire. The federal armories at Springfield and Harpers Ferry develop true interchangeable parts over the following decades.

The result is the 'American system of manufacturing' — mass production using interchangeable parts and specialized machines. It will make everything from clocks to sewing machines to, eventually, automobiles. Whitney doesn't invent the factory. But he sells the dream of it.

quiz.question: Why was Whitney broke despite inventing the cotton gin?
options: He sold the patent too cheaply
The gin didn't work as advertised
Southern planters pirated his design and he lost years in fruitless lawsuits
quiz.explanation: The gin was so simple to copy that planters built their own, and Southern courts were unsympathetic to Whitney's patent claims.

quiz.question: What was revolutionary about Whitney's musket manufacturing concept?
options: He used steam power to make them faster
Standardized interchangeable parts that any worker could assemble
He invented a new type of gunpowder
He hired enslaved workers to reduce costs
quiz.explanation: Instead of hand-crafted unique weapons, Whitney proposed standardized parts — a broken component could be swapped with any identical replacement.

quiz.question: What broader manufacturing revolution did interchangeable parts enable?
options: The American system of mass production
quiz.explanation: The 'American system of manufacturing' used interchangeable parts and specialized machines to mass-produce everything from clocks to firearms.

quiz.question: How many muskets did Whitney promise the government, and how late was he?
quiz.explanation: Whitney promised 10,000 muskets in two years but delivered them eight years late — the concept was easier to pitch than to execute.

nodes.narrative:
Whitney's interchangeable parts are reshaping manufacturing in the East. Meanwhile, Jefferson has just bought 828,000 square miles of territory that no American has ever mapped — and he needs someone to go look at it.

1804. Meriwether Lewis, Jefferson's personal secretary, and William Clark, a frontier soldier, set out from Camp Dubois near St. Louis with about 45 men — soldiers, hunters, interpreters, and Clark's enslaved man York. Their mission: find a water route to the Pacific Ocean, document the plants, animals, and peoples of the West, and establish American claims to the territory.

The journey is staggering. They paddle and portage up the Missouri River, cross the Rocky Mountains on horseback, and canoe down the Columbia to the Pacific. They cover over 8,000 miles in 28 months. They encounter grizzly bears, nearly starve in the Bitterroot Mountains, and survive only because a Shoshone woman named Sacagawea — carrying her infant son — guides them, translates, and vouches for their peaceful intentions to suspicious tribes.

They reach the Pacific in November 1805. Clark carves into a tree: 'William Clark December 3rd 1805. By land from the U. States in 1804 & 1805.'

The expedition returns to St. Louis in September 1806 to a heroes' welcome — most had assumed they were dead. They bring back 178 new plant species, 122 new animal species, and detailed maps of territory that will become a dozen states.

Lewis and Clark don't find the hoped-for water route to the Pacific — it doesn't exist. But they prove that an overland crossing is possible, and they establish the American claim to the Oregon Country. The West is no longer a rumor. It's a destination.

quiz.question: Why was Sacagawea essential to the expedition's success?
options: She guided, translated, and vouched for the explorers' peaceful intentions to Native tribes
quiz.explanation: Sacagawea's presence with her infant son signaled peaceful intent, and her knowledge of languages and terrain proved invaluable.

quiz.question: How far did Lewis and Clark travel on their expedition?
quiz.explanation: The expedition covered over 8,000 miles round-trip in 28 months — from St. Louis to the Pacific and back.

quiz.question: What did the expedition fail to find?
options: An all-water route to the Pacific Ocean
Any new plant or animal species
quiz.explanation: Jefferson hoped for a 'Northwest Passage' — a continuous water route to the Pacific — but the Rocky Mountains proved this was impossible.

quiz.question: How many new species did Lewis and Clark document?
quiz.explanation: The expedition documented 178 new plant species and 122 new animal species — an enormous contribution to natural science.

nodes.narrative:
Lewis and Clark have proven you can cross the continent on foot, by horse, and by canoe. But Robert Fulton is about to prove you can go upstream — and it will change everything.

1807. For all of human history, river travel has been a one-way affair. You can float downstream easily enough, but going upstream means poling, rowing, or hauling your boat with ropes from shore. A flatboat from Pittsburgh to New Orleans takes about six weeks. The return trip — upstream — takes four to five months, if you bother at all. Most boatmen sell their vessels for lumber and walk home.

Robert Fulton, an American artist-turned-engineer, has been obsessing over steam power for years. Working with a British-built engine, he constructs the North River Steamboat — later nicknamed the Clermont. On August 17, 1807, the boat chugs up the Hudson River from New York City to Albany: 150 miles in 32 hours.

Onlookers are stunned. Some think the boat is on fire. Others predict it will explode. It doesn't. And the world changes.

Within a decade, steamboats are churning up and down the Mississippi, the Ohio, and every navigable river in America. The trip from New Orleans to Louisville drops from three months to two weeks. Goods, people, and ideas now flow both ways. The interior of the continent — all those acres Jefferson just bought — is suddenly accessible.

Fulton doesn't invent the steamboat — others built prototypes before him. But he builds the first one that actually makes money. And in America, that's what counts.

quiz.question: What fundamental problem did the steamboat solve for river commerce?
options: Rivers were too shallow for large boats
Going upstream was impossibly slow — taking months vs. weeks downstream
There were no bridges to cross rivers
quiz.explanation: Before steamboats, floating downstream was easy but the return upstream took months — most boatmen simply walked home.

quiz.question: What was significant about Fulton's achievement compared to earlier steamboat attempts?
options: He was the first to try a steamboat
He built the first commercially successful steamboat
quiz.explanation: Others built prototypes, but Fulton's was the first to actually turn a profit — proving the technology was commercially viable.

quiz.question: How long did the Clermont take to travel 150 miles up the Hudson River?
quiz.explanation: The Clermont made the 150-mile journey from New York City to Albany in 32 hours — astonishing for upstream travel.

quiz.question: How did steamboats connect to the Louisiana Purchase?
options: They made the interior Jefferson bought suddenly accessible for two-way commerce
Jefferson used a steamboat to negotiate the purchase
Steamboats carried the purchase money to France
quiz.explanation: Steamboats transformed the vast Louisiana Territory from inaccessible wilderness into commercially viable land by enabling upstream river travel.

nodes.narrative:
Fulton's steamboat has connected the interior. Whitney's gin and interchangeable parts have transformed agriculture and manufacturing. Now the War of 1812 forces the young republic to put all its innovations to the ultimate test.

1812. When war breaks out with Britain, the United States has a tiny navy of 17 ships against the Royal Navy's 600. American factories are primitive. The national army numbers barely 12,000 men. On paper, this contest should be over in weeks.

But American technology and ingenuity level the playing field in surprising ways. The USS Constitution — 'Old Ironsides' — defeats the HMS Guerriere in a ship-to-ship battle that stuns the world. British cannonballs literally bounce off her oak hull, reinforced with a copper bottom designed by Paul Revere's foundry.

On the Great Lakes, American shipbuilders construct an entire fleet from scratch in the wilderness. Oliver Hazard Perry builds nine warships at Erie, Pennsylvania, from green timber cut from the surrounding forests. His fleet defeats the British at the Battle of Lake Erie, giving America control of the crucial waterway. Perry's famous dispatch reads: 'We have met the enemy and they are ours.'

Even in defeat, innovation shows. When the British burn Washington, the Americans rebuild faster than anyone expects — a testament to the growing industrial capacity of the young nation. And Whitney's armories at Springfield keep producing muskets with interchangeable parts, ensuring the army never runs out of weapons.

The War of 1812 proves that American innovation isn't just about comfort and commerce — it's a strategic advantage. The industrial foundation being laid in these years will, within fifty years, make the United States a manufacturing superpower.

quiz.question: Why was the USS Constitution nicknamed 'Old Ironsides'?
options: She was built from iron plates
British cannonballs bounced off her reinforced oak hull
She was the oldest ship in the Navy
quiz.explanation: Her thick live-oak hull and copper bottom (from Paul Revere's foundry) were so tough that cannonballs seemed to bounce off — earning the nickname.

quiz.question: What was remarkable about the American fleet on the Great Lakes?
options: It was captured from the British
It was built from scratch in the wilderness using green timber from surrounding forests
It was powered by steam engines
quiz.explanation: Perry built nine warships at Erie from freshly cut timber — an extraordinary feat of frontier engineering.

quiz.question: What was Oliver Hazard Perry's famous dispatch after the Battle of Lake Erie?
options: 'I have not yet begun to fight'
'We have met the enemy and they are ours'
quiz.explanation: Perry's message — 'We have met the enemy and they are ours' — became one of the most famous dispatches in naval history.

quiz.question: How did American industrial innovation provide a strategic advantage during the war?
options: Steam-powered warships dominated the British fleet
Whitney's armories with interchangeable parts kept the army supplied with muskets
American railroads moved troops faster than British ships
quiz.explanation: Whitney's system of interchangeable parts meant muskets could be mass-produced and repaired in the field — a crucial advantage.
